title: A Fine Balance
author: Rohinton Mistry
desc: Four strangers thrown together in Bombay during Indira Gandhi's 1975 Emergency. Long but utterly absorbing — one of the great novels about India's poor. Heartbreaking and deeply human.

title: A Gentleman in Moscow
author: Amor Towles
desc: A Russian count sentenced to lifelong house arrest in a grand Moscow hotel in 1922. What unfolds is a warm, witty, beautifully observed novel about how to live with grace. Possibly the most enjoyable literary novel of the 2010s.

title: A Long Way Gone
author: Ishmael Beah
desc: Beah was 12 when Sierra Leone's civil war took his family. By 13 he was a child soldier. This clear-eyed, devastating memoir about how an ordinary boy was turned into a killer — and then rehabilitated.

title: A Passage to India
author: E.M. Forster
desc: A British woman visits colonial India and an accusation explodes across the racial divide. Forster's masterpiece on empire, friendship and cultural misunderstanding is eerily prescient.

title: A Short History of Nearly Everything
author: Bill Bryson
desc: Bryson surveys the history of science — physics, chemistry, geology, biology — by asking how we know what we know. Funny, awe-inspiring and genuinely humbling. Won the Aventis Prize. One of the best popular science books ever written.

title: A Thousand Splendid Suns
author: Khaled Hosseini
desc: Two Afghan women from different generations, forced together as wives to the same abusive man, find in each other the strength to survive — across decades of war and Taliban rule. Even more devastating than The Kite Runner.

title: All Played Out
author: Pete Davies
desc: Pete Davies was given complete inside access to the England squad at the 1990 World Cup in Italy — living in the hotel, travelling with the players, interviewing Gazza, Robson, Lineker and the rest as the tournament unfolded. The book that invented English football writing. Gazza's tears in Turin are here, the penalty shootout loss to Germany, the hooligans, the tabloid circus. Reissued in 2010 as 'One Night in Turin' to coincide with the documentary. Essential World Cup reading — perfectly timed for right now.

title: Americanah
author: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie
desc: Ifemelu leaves Nigeria for America — a love story that becomes a sharp, funny, essential examination of race and identity. What it means to be African in the West, seen from both sides. Adichie is one of the most important writers alive.

title: And Then There Were None
author: Agatha Christie
desc: Ten strangers are lured to an isolated island and begin to die one by one. The world's best-selling mystery novel — 100 million copies sold. Christie at her most brilliant and most terrifying. Reads in one sitting and you will not guess the ending.

title: Bel Canto
author: Ann Patchett
desc: Terrorists take hostages at a South American dinner party and over weeks, extraordinary bonds form between captors and captives — with an opera singer at the heart of it all. Warm, unexpected and unlike any other novel.

title: Beloved
author: Toni Morrison
desc: A former enslaved woman in Ohio is haunted — literally — by the ghost of her dead daughter. Morrison's Pulitzer Prize-winning masterpiece about the psychic wounds of slavery. One of the most important American novels ever written. Profoundly moving.

title: Between the World and Me
author: Ta-Nehisi Coates
desc: Written as a letter to his teenage son, Coates meditates on what it means to be Black in America — the fear that lives in the body, the history of plunder. National Book Award winner.

title: Born a Crime
author: Trevor Noah
desc: Trevor Noah grew up under apartheid as a mixed-race child — literally a criminal by law. His memoir is laugh-out-loud funny and heartbreaking in equal measure. Possibly the most entertaining memoir written this century. Essential reading about apartheid from the inside.

title: Captain Corelli's Mandolin
author: Louis de Bernières
desc: On the Greek island of Cephallonia during the Italian and German occupation of WW2, an Italian officer with a mandolin falls in love with a local girl. Rich, funny, romantic, devastating — one of the great sweeping novels of the 1990s. Hugely readable and deeply human.

title: Catch-22
author: Joseph Heller
desc: Captain Yossarian tries to get out of flying WW2 missions by proving he's insane — but wanting to survive proves you're sane. The great American satire on war and bureaucracy. Hilarious and horrifying in equal measure.

title: Cloudstreet
author: Tim Winton
desc: Two working-class families share a large house in postwar Perth over twenty years. Australia's best-loved novel — sprawling, funny, heartbreaking and utterly real. Winton captures the Australian spirit better than anyone. Miles Franklin Award winner.

title: Convenience Store Woman
author: Sayaka Murata
desc: Keiko is 36 and has worked in the same convenience store for 18 years — and she is perfectly happy with this. A quietly radical novel about conformity, neurodiversity and social pressure. Funny, weird and very short. Couldn't be more Japanese.

title: Crime and Punishment
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
desc: A destitute student in St Petersburg convinces himself he has the right to commit murder — and then lives with the consequences. One of the great psychological novels of all time. More gripping than its reputation suggests — essentially a thriller.

title: Cutting for Stone
author: Abraham Verghese
desc: Twin boys are born in an Addis Ababa mission hospital — their birth kills their mother and drives their father away — and the novel follows them across Ethiopia's history of revolution, civil war and exodus to New York. Verghese writes about medicine and the body with extraordinary beauty. Gripping from the first page. Ethiopia is one of the world's oldest civilisations and entirely absent from most reading lists.

title: Disgrace
author: J.M. Coetzee
desc: A Cape Town professor falls from grace after an affair with a student, then retreats to his daughter's rural farm where something terrible happens. Short, brutal and morally complex — Coetzee's most piercing novel about post-apartheid South Africa.

title: Educated
author: Tara Westover
desc: A woman who grew up in a survivalist Idaho family — never attending school — eventually earns a PhD from Cambridge. About family, knowledge and the courage to think for yourself. The most compelling memoir of recent years.

title: Exit West
author: Mohsin Hamid
desc: In a country on the brink of civil war, two people fall in love. Then magic doors appear — stepping through transports refugees across the world. Beautiful, urgent and achingly human. Short and essential reading right now.

title: Factfulness
author: Hans Rosling
desc: Ten reasons we're wrong about the world — and why things are better than you think. Rosling uses data to demolish our instinctive pessimism. Bill Gates called it one of the most important books he'd ever read. Genuinely changes how you see the news.

title: Flowers for Algernon
author: Daniel Keyes
desc: Charlie Gordon has an IQ of 68 and undergoes an experimental procedure that triples his intelligence — told through his diary entries. One of the most emotional science fiction novels ever written. Will make you cry. Guaranteed.

title: Football Against the Enemy
author: Simon Kuper
desc: Kuper travels to 22 countries to discover what football reveals about the places it is played. From Brazil's fan clubs to Cameroon's football politics, Dutch cruyffism to Scottish sectarianism and a Kenyan league match at the edge of the Rift Valley. Uses the game as a lens on culture, identity and power in the way the best travel writing uses place. The book that established literary football journalism — and the one football book that genuinely fits alongside everything else in this collection.

title: Good Omens
author: Terry Pratchett & Neil Gaiman
desc: An angel and a demon who've been friends since the Garden of Eden team up to prevent the apocalypse because they've grown rather fond of Earth. Brilliantly funny, warmly human and completely irresistible. The best comedy fantasy novel ever written.

title: Guns, Germs, and Steel
author: Jared Diamond
desc: Why did some civilisations conquer others? Diamond argues the answer lies in geography and environment, not race or intelligence. Pulitzer Prize winner. Eye-opening and endlessly debated — changes how you see the whole of human history.

title: Half of a Yellow Sun
author: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie
desc: Nigeria in the 1960s as the devastating Biafra war tears the country apart. Orange Prize winner. Adichie makes history feel personal and urgent.

title: Hamnet
author: Maggie O'Farrell
desc: Plague arrives in 1580s Stratford-upon-Avon and a boy called Hamnet dies. His father is a glover who writes plays. O'Farrell's devastating reimagining of Shakespeare's family and loss. Women's Prize winner.

title: Hiroshima
author: John Hersey
desc: Six survivors of the atomic bomb tell their stories in Hersey's landmark work of journalism. Originally took up an entire issue of The New Yorker. Short, essential and will permanently change how you understand Hiroshima. One of the greatest pieces of reporting ever written.

title: Homegoing
author: Yaa Gyasi
desc: Two half-sisters in 18th-century Ghana — one marries a British coloniser, one is sold into slavery — and the novel follows their descendants across 300 years. Each chapter is a different generation. Gripping, heartbreaking, and teaches you everything about the slave trade.

title: How Soccer Explains the World
author: Franklin Foer
desc: Foer travels from the ultranationalist ultras of Red Star Belgrade to the oligarchs of Chelsea, from the Catalan separatism of Barcelona to the class politics of Glasgow's Old Firm — using football clubs as a lens on globalisation, tribalism and nationalism. Each chapter is essentially a country study in disguise. Slightly dated in places (2004) but the core argument — that football reflects and amplifies the tensions of the modern world — has only grown more relevant.

title: I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings
author: Maya Angelou
desc: Maya Angelou's memoir of childhood in the American South — racial humiliation, trauma and the transcendence of literature. One of the great American voices, on one of the most important subjects. Beautiful, warm and essential.

title: In the Time of the Butterflies
author: Julia Alvarez
desc: The true story of the four Mirabal sisters who stood up against the Trujillo dictatorship. Beautifully written and compulsively readable — one of the great political novels of the Americas.

title: Invisible Man
author: Ralph Ellison
desc: A young Black man narrates his journey from the South to Harlem, discovering that society refuses to see him as a person. One of the most important American novels ever written. National Book Award winner.

title: Kindred
author: Octavia E. Butler
desc: A modern Black woman is repeatedly pulled back in time to a pre-Civil War Maryland plantation, where she must keep a white slave owner alive to ensure her own existence. Butler's masterpiece — about race, power and survival. Impossible to put down and impossible to forget.

title: Kitchen
author: Banana Yoshimoto
desc: Mikage, newly orphaned, is taken in by a boy and his transgender mother and finds solace in kitchens. Two novellas about grief, food and finding home. Gentle, warm and deeply Japanese. Reads in one sitting.

title: Kokoro
author: Natsume Soseki
desc: A young man befriends an enigmatic older man called Sensei who is hiding a secret from his past. Japan's most beloved novel — about guilt, loneliness and the end of the Meiji era. Short, beautiful and strangely modern.

title: Like Water for Chocolate
author: Laura Esquivel
desc: Tita, youngest daughter in a Mexican family, is forbidden to marry because tradition demands she care for her mother. Her emotions literally infuse the food she cooks. Magical, sensuous and deeply romantic. One of the most loved Latin American novels.

title: Lincoln in the Bardo
author: George Saunders
desc: President Lincoln sits with his dead 11-year-old son while ghosts swirl around the Washington cemetery. Told entirely through historical quotes and ghost voices. Booker Prize winner.

title: Little Fires Everywhere
author: Celeste Ng
desc: Two families collide in a picture-perfect Ohio suburb — the rule-following Richardsons and the free-spirited Warrens — when a custody battle exposes what lies beneath. Sharp, gripping and impossible to put down.

title: Love in the Time of Cholera
author: Gabriel García Márquez
desc: Florentino Ariza waits over fifty years for Fermina Daza, the woman he loves who chose another man. García Márquez's most romantic novel — a meditation on time, obsession and what love actually means.

title: Midnight's Children
author: Salman Rushdie
desc: Born at the moment of Indian independence, Saleem Sinai discovers he has telepathic links to the 1,001 other children born that night. An exhilarating, comic and deeply Indian novel. Booker of Bookers.

title: My Brilliant Friend
author: Elena Ferrante
desc: A ferocious, intimate story of female friendship set in a poor Naples neighbourhood from the 1950s. Compulsively readable — named NYT's #1 book of the 21st century. If you read one novel this year, make it this one.

title: Nervous Conditions
author: Tsitsi Dangarembga
desc: Tambu, a young girl in rural Rhodesia, wins a place at a mission school and begins navigating the competing demands of colonial education, tradition and family. The founding novel of Zimbabwean literature in English — warm, readable and quietly radical. One of the BBC's 100 Novels That Shaped Our World.

title: Never Let Me Go
author: Kazuo Ishiguro
desc: Kathy H looks back on growing up in an idyllic English boarding school. Quietly, slowly, you realise something is deeply wrong. Ishiguro's most haunting novel — about memory, mortality and what it means to be human. Reads in two sittings.

title: Normal People
author: Sally Rooney
desc: Connell and Marianne from a small Irish town start a secret relationship, then collide repeatedly at Trinity College Dublin. About class, communication and how two people can simultaneously fail and complete each other. Enormously compulsive.

title: Norwegian Wood
author: Haruki Murakami
desc: A coming-of-age story set in 1960s Tokyo — Toru falls in love with a beautiful, damaged girl called Naoko. Murakami's most accessible and emotional novel. Beautifully sad, utterly readable and the best place to start with Japan's greatest living writer.

title: One Hundred Years of Solitude
author: Gabriel García Márquez
desc: The Buendía family saga through seven generations in the fictional town of Macondo. The father of magic realism. Nobel Prize winner. More accessible than its reputation suggests.

title: Pachinko
author: Min Jin Lee
desc: A Korean family across four generations living as outsiders in Japan — from the 1910s occupation to 1980s Osaka. About discrimination, sacrifice and survival. One of the most talked-about novels of the decade. Utterly absorbing.

title: Pedro Páramo
author: Juan Rulfo
desc: A man travels to the Mexican ghost town of Comala to find his father, Pedro Páramo — and finds only the dead. Only 124 pages, but García Márquez said he read it hundreds of times and could not have written One Hundred Years of Solitude without it. The foundation stone of Latin American magical realism. Strange, hypnotic and completely unlike anything else.

title: Persepolis
author: Marjane Satrapi
desc: A graphic memoir of growing up in Iran during the Islamic Revolution. Satrapi is funny, honest and heartbreaking. Reads in a sitting and teaches you more about Iran than any news report. Named on multiple best-of-century lists.

title: Piranesi
author: Susanna Clarke
desc: A man lives alone in a labyrinthine House of infinite marble halls and tidal statues, writing meticulous journals. Strange, beautiful and deeply original — a mystery that reveals itself slowly. Won the Women's Prize for Fiction. Reads in one sitting.

title: Prisoners of Geography
author: Tim Marshall
desc: Ten maps, ten chapters, ten explanations for why the world works the way it does. Why Russia is obsessed with Ukraine. Why China claims the South China Sea. Why Africa's borders cause endless conflict. Why the Arctic is the next great geopolitical flashpoint. Marshall is a foreign correspondent — this reads like good journalism, not a textbook. Short chapters, completely accessible, and permanently changes how you read the news.

title: River Town
author: Peter Hessler
desc: Two years teaching English in a small Chinese Yangtze river town in the late 1990s. Intimate, funny and humanising — a portrait of ordinary Chinese life that no news report could capture. Hessler is the best writer on China in English.

title: Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind
author: Yuval Noah Harari
desc: A grand survey of human history from prehistoric Homo sapiens to the present — covering the agricultural revolution, empires, money, religion and the future. Controversial in its sweep but endlessly thought-provoking. One of the most widely read nonfiction books of the decade.

title: Season of Migration to the North
author: Tayeb Salih
desc: A Sudanese man returns from studying in England and discovers a mysterious neighbour who tells a dark story of his own time in Europe. The Arab world's most important novel — a postcolonial masterpiece. Short and stunning.

title: Shuggie Bain
author: Douglas Stuart
desc: A young boy in 1980s Glasgow tries to save his beloved, alcoholic mother from herself as Thatcher's deindustrialisation destroys their community. Booker Prize winner. Devastating, compassionate and brilliantly specific about place and class.

title: Slaughterhouse-Five
author: Kurt Vonnegut
desc: Billy Pilgrim, a WW2 POW, becomes unstuck in time and keeps revisiting the firebombing of Dresden. One of the great anti-war novels — astonishingly funny. Short, strange and utterly unforgettable.

title: Small Island
author: Andrea Levy
desc: Two Jamaican immigrants arrive in post-war London expecting the mother country — and find something very different. Whitbread Prize winner. Funny, warm and devastating — the essential novel of Caribbean-British experience.

title: Small Things Like These
author: Claire Keegan
desc: A coal merchant in a small Irish town in 1985 discovers the dark secret of the local convent. Reads in a single sitting — only 120 pages — but lingers for weeks. Claire Keegan is a master of the short form. About moral courage in the face of complicity.

title: Snow
author: Orhan Pamuk
desc: A poet returns to Turkey after years in exile and is snowbound in a remote city at the heart of political and religious conflict. Pamuk's most accessible novel. Nobel Prize winner.

title: So Long a Letter
author: Mariama Bâ
desc: A Senegalese widow writes a long letter to her best friend after her husband takes a second young wife. One of Africa's most important feminist novels. Only 90 pages. Essential.

title: Soccernomics
author: Simon Kuper & Stefan Szymanski
desc: Why does England always lose penalty shootouts? Why do smaller, poorer nations sometimes outperform richer ones? Why do clubs consistently overpay for strikers? Kuper and economist Szymanski apply data and behavioural economics to football and reach genuinely surprising conclusions. Originally published in the UK as 'Why England Lose'. Funny, accessible and revelatory — reads like Freakonomics for the beautiful game. An updated World Cup edition exists for 2026.

title: Station Eleven
author: Emily St. John Mandel
desc: A flu pandemic kills most of the world's population. Twenty years later, a travelling Shakespeare company moves between settlements. Elegiac, beautiful and unexpectedly hopeful. One of the best novels about what we'd lose — and what we'd keep.

title: Stoner
author: John Williams
desc: The quiet, largely uneventful life of a Missouri English professor — his failed marriage, his one great love, his career frustrations. Nothing happens and yet it is one of the most moving novels ever written. Called the saddest book in the world.

title: Suite Française
author: Irène Némirovsky
desc: Two novellas about France under Nazi occupation — the panicked exodus from Paris in 1940, then the slow strange life of a village billeting German soldiers. Némirovsky was writing this as it happened; she was deported to Auschwitz in 1942 and murdered there. The manuscript was hidden in a suitcase by her daughters for 60 years. One of the most extraordinary literary documents of the 20th century — and a genuinely brilliant novel too.

title: The Absolutely True Diary of a Part-Time Indian
author: Sherman Alexie
desc: Arnold Spirit Jr leaves his Native American reservation to attend the all-white school in a nearby town. Funny, heartbreaking and illustrated with stick figures. The essential novel about Native American teenage life.

title: The Alchemist
author: Paulo Coelho
desc: A short, lyrical fable about a Spanish shepherd boy travelling to Egypt in search of treasure. The world's most translated novel after the Bible. Quick to read, universally loved.

title: The Arabian Nights (One Thousand and One Nights)
author: Various (traditional)
desc: Scheherazade tells stories every night to stay alive — and the stories sprawl across sailors, thieves, genies, sultans and magical worlds. The original anthology of world storytelling. Read Husain Haddawy's translation for maximum pleasure.

title: The Autobiography of Malcolm X
author: Malcolm X & Alex Haley
desc: Malcolm X traces his path from ghetto hustler to Nation of Islam leader to global human rights figure. One of the great American autobiographies — gripping, angry and honest. Transforms how you understand American racism.

title: The Bell Jar
author: Sylvia Plath
desc: Esther Greenwood, a brilliant young writer, wins a New York internship and then spirals into depression. Plath's semi-autobiographical novel is one of the most honest accounts of mental illness ever written — and also funny and keenly observed.

title: The Boat
author: Nam Le
desc: Seven stories spanning continents and cultures — a Vietnamese-Australian with a refugee father, a teenage hitman in Colombia, an elderly painter in Hiroshima. The title story about Vietnamese boat people is unforgettable.

title: The Body: A Guide for Occupants
author: Bill Bryson
desc: Bryson's tour of the human body — from bacteria to the brain to why we age — is laugh-out-loud funny and genuinely astonishing. The best science writing available: rigorous, accessible and endlessly entertaining.

title: The Book Thief
author: Markus Zusak
desc: Narrated by Death, this is the story of Liesel, a young girl in Nazi Germany who steals books and shares them with her neighbours during Allied bombing raids. Written by an Australian author. Deeply moving and beautifully told — one of the most loved novels of the 21st century.

title: The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
author: Junot Díaz
desc: An overweight Dominican-American nerd obsessed with sci-fi tries to find love while his family carries the shadow of Trujillo's dictatorship. Funny, heartbreaking and linguistically electric. Pulitzer Prize winner.

title: The Cairo Trilogy: Palace Walk
author: Naguib Mahfouz
desc: The first volume of Mahfouz's Nobel Prize-winning trilogy — a Cairo merchant family in the 1910s during British occupation. Egypt's most beloved novels. Rich, human and deeply immersive. The Arab world's answer to Tolstoy.

title: The Color Purple
author: Alice Walker
desc: Celie, a Black woman in 1930s Georgia, writes letters to God and to her sister as she endures abuse and slowly finds her voice and dignity. Pulitzer Prize winner. Raw, heartbreaking and ultimately triumphant — one of the great American novels.

title: The Count of Monte Cristo
author: Alexandre Dumas
desc: Edmond Dantès is falsely imprisoned and emerges years later as the mysterious, fabulously wealthy Count of Monte Cristo — with a single purpose: revenge against those who destroyed his life. The greatest adventure novel ever written. Full unabridged edition is 1276 pages, but the Penguin Classics abridged edition (~500 pages) is how most readers approach it and loses nothing essential. Once started, essentially impossible to stop.

title: The Curious Incident of the Dog in the Night-Time
author: Mark Haddon
desc: Christopher, 15, who has a condition like autism, investigates the murder of his neighbour's dog. Narrated with deadpan logic and unexpected humour. One of the most original voices in fiction — funny, moving and impossible to put down.

title: The Diary of a Young Girl
author: Anne Frank
desc: Anne Frank's diary of two years hiding with her family in Amsterdam during the Nazi occupation. One of the most widely read books in human history — because Anne's voice is so alive, funny and unbearably real. Everyone should read this.

title: The Feast of the Goat
author: Mario Vargas Llosa
desc: The last day of Trujillo's dictatorship in the Dominican Republic — told from three perspectives including the dictator himself. Vargas Llosa at his most gripping. A Nobel laureate's most political and propulsive novel.

title: The Forty Rules of Love
author: Elif Shafak
desc: A bored housewife in Massachusetts reads a novel about the mystical friendship between the poet Rumi and the wandering dervish Shams of Tabriz — and both stories transform each other. Elif Shafak's most beloved novel. Warm, spiritual and deeply readable.

title: The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo
author: Stieg Larsson
desc: Journalist Mikael Blomkvist and hacker Lisbeth Salander investigate a decades-old disappearance in a wealthy Swedish family. The novel that launched Scandinavian crime fiction globally — gripping, politically sharp and impossible to put down.

title: The Glass Castle
author: Jeannette Walls
desc: Jeannette Walls grew up in a chaotic, nomadic family with brilliant but deeply irresponsible parents — no stable home, often no food — and eventually made it to New York. Compulsively readable and very funny despite everything. One of the great American memoirs.

title: The Glass Palace
author: Amitav Ghosh
desc: Beginning with the fall of Burma's last king in 1885, Ghosh follows three families across a century of colonialism, war and independence across South-East Asia. Epic but readable.

title: The God of Small Things
author: Arundhati Roy
desc: A gorgeous, devastating novel about family, forbidden love and caste in Kerala. Roy's prose is lush and inventive. Booker Prize winner. Not difficult reading — just very rich.

title: The Grapes of Wrath
author: John Steinbeck
desc: The Joad family flee the Oklahoma Dust Bowl to California in search of work and dignity during the Great Depression. Pulitzer Prize winner. One of the great American novels about poverty, displacement and the resilience of ordinary people.

title: The Handmaid's Tale
author: Margaret Atwood
desc: In the Republic of Gilead — formerly the USA — women are the property of the state. Offred is a Handmaid assigned to bear children for a Commander. Atwood's chilling masterwork feels more relevant with every passing year.

title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
author: Douglas Adams
desc: Moments before Earth is demolished to make way for a hyperspace bypass, Arthur Dent is whisked off the planet by his alien friend Ford Prefect. The funniest, most quotable sci-fi novel ever written — and one of the best comedies in any genre. Reads in one sitting.

title: The House of the Spirits
author: Isabel Allende
desc: A sweeping family saga across three generations in Chile, ending with the 1973 coup. Magic realism with a unique Latin American female voice. Allende's most beloved novel — gripping, romantic and politically essential.

title: The Hundred-Year-Old Man Who Climbed Out the Window and Disappeared
author: Jonas Jonasson
desc: Allan Karlsson, 100 years old, climbs out of his nursing home window and accidentally gets tangled in a criminal adventure — while flashbacks reveal he somehow blundered into almost every major event of the 20th century, from the Manhattan Project to Stalin's inner circle to Mao's China. Hilarious in the way Forrest Gump is but smarter. Pure fun — and you absorb an enormous amount of 20th century history without noticing.

title: The Inheritance of Loss
author: Kiran Desai
desc: An elderly judge, his orphaned granddaughter and her tutor live in the Himalayas as a separatist insurgency rages around them. Booker Prize winner. About colonialism, immigration and the impossible question of belonging.

title: The Joy Luck Club
author: Amy Tan
desc: Four Chinese immigrant women and their American-born daughters — their secrets, their clashing worlds and what gets lost in translation between generations. Tender, funny and heartbreaking.

title: The Kite Runner
author: Khaled Hosseini
desc: A story of friendship, betrayal and redemption set against Afghanistan's history from the 1970s through the Taliban era. Gripping and emotional — gives you an intimate window into a country most of us only know from headlines.

title: The Left Hand of Darkness
author: Ursula K. Le Guin
desc: A human envoy travels to a planet where the inhabitants have no fixed gender. Le Guin's most celebrated novel — a deeply intelligent exploration of gender, politics and what it means to be human. Hugo and Nebula Award winner.

title: The Lover
author: Marguerite Duras
desc: A teenage French girl in colonial Vietnam begins an affair with a wealthy Chinese man. Duras's autobiographical novella is short, sensuous and unforgettable — one of the great French novels of the 20th century and a rare literary window into Indochina. Prix Goncourt winner. Read in one sitting.

title: The Luminaries
author: Eleanor Catton
desc: A murder mystery set on New Zealand's South Island during the 1866 gold rush. Booker Prize winner — the youngest ever at 28. Long but structured by astrology and genuinely thrilling.

title: The Master and Margarita
author: Mikhail Bulgakov
desc: The Devil visits Stalin's Moscow and chaos — miraculous and horrifying — follows. Simultaneously a savage satire on Soviet life, a love story and a retelling of Pilate's encounter with Jesus. Russia's most beloved novel.

title: The Memory Police
author: Yoko Ogawa
desc: On a nameless island, objects keep disappearing — roses, birds, maps — and with them, all memory of their existence. A haunting, gentle dystopia about loss and erasure. Ogawa is one of Japan's great writers. Quietly unforgettable.

title: The Midnight Library
author: Matt Haig
desc: Nora Seed finds a library between life and death containing every book about every life she could have lived. Warm, genuinely moving and openly about mental health and the will to live. One of the most widely read novels of recent years.

title: The Name of the Rose
author: Umberto Eco
desc: A Franciscan monk and his novice investigate a series of mysterious deaths in a 14th-century Italian abbey. A medieval murder mystery, a philosophical puzzle and a love letter to books. Surprisingly gripping.

title: The Namesake
author: Jhumpa Lahiri
desc: The Ganguli family emigrate from Calcutta to Boston. Their son Gogol grows up caught between two worlds. Lahiri's warm, precise novel about immigration, identity and family. Beautifully easy to read while carrying real depth.

title: The Narrow Road to the Deep North
author: Richard Flanagan
desc: An Australian surgeon survives the Death Railway in Burma as a POW, and lives with it for the rest of his life. Booker Prize winner. Flanagan is Australia's greatest living novelist. About love, war and what we carry — devastating and beautiful.

title: The No. 1 Ladies' Detective Agency
author: Alexander McCall Smith
desc: Precious Ramotswe — 'traditionally built' and enormously wise — sets up Botswana's first female detective agency and solves the small mysteries of her community with warmth, good sense and tea. Joyful, funny and somehow teaches you more about Botswana's landscape, culture and optimism than any serious novel. A deliberate antidote to heavy reading — and a genuine portrait of a country rarely seen in fiction.

title: The Old Man and the Sea
author: Ernest Hemingway
desc: An aged Cuban fisherman heads alone into the deep sea and hooks a great marlin. Hemingway's shortest, most perfect novel. Pulitzer Prize winner. Reads in two hours. About dignity, struggle and ageing.

title: The Patience of Stone
author: Atiq Rahimi
desc: In an unnamed war zone, a woman tends to her comatose husband and slowly begins to speak her silenced truths to him. Winner of France's Prix Goncourt. Only 115 pages. Haunting, brave and unlike anything else about Afghanistan.

title: The Poet X
author: Elizabeth Acevedo
desc: Xiomara Batista writes poetry to survive her strict Dominican-American home and first love. Written entirely in verse — but reads faster than prose. Carnegie Medal and National Book Award winner. One of the most alive, urgent voices in contemporary literature.

title: The Posthumous Memoirs of Brás Cubas
author: Joaquim Maria Machado de Assis
desc: A dead man narrates his own life — with digressions, contradictions and jokes — in 19th-century Rio de Janeiro. Wildly ahead of its time and hilarious. Brazil's greatest novelist, unknown in the English-speaking world but championed by Susan Sontag.

title: The Princess Bride
author: William Goldman
desc: Fencing, fighting, torture, revenge, giants, monsters, chases, escapes, true love, miracles — Goldman's novel is all of these. Funnier and stranger than the film it inspired, with a meta-fictional framing that makes it unlike any other adventure story.

title: The Reader
author: Bernhard Schlink
desc: A fifteen-year-old boy in post-war Germany has an affair with an older woman who disappears from his life — then reappears years later as a defendant in a war crimes trial. Short, gripping, morally complex. Schlink forces you to sit inside Germany's impossible post-war question: how do you reckon with a parent generation of perpetrators? The first German novel to reach number one on the New York Times bestseller list.

title: The Reluctant Fundamentalist
author: Mohsin Hamid
desc: A Pakistani man who loved America sits with an American in a Lahore café and tells his story — his rise on Wall Street, then 9/11, then the reckoning. Short, gripping and clever.

title: The Remains of the Day
author: Kazuo Ishiguro
desc: An English butler takes a road trip and quietly reflects on a life of service, missed chances and unspoken love. Devastating in its restraint. Booker Prize winner. Nobel laureate Ishiguro at his most precise — and most moving.

title: The Secret History
author: Donna Tartt
desc: A group of classics students at a Vermont college commit a murder — and this novel begins by telling you so on page one. Dark academia before dark academia existed. Incredibly compulsive and beautifully written.

title: The Secret River
author: Kate Grenville
desc: William Thornhill, transported from London to New South Wales as a convict, tries to build a life on a stretch of river already inhabited by the Darug people. Grenville tells the story from both sides — settler and Indigenous — with unflinching honesty about what colonisation actually meant on the ground. Commonwealth Writers Prize winner. Essential reading for understanding Australia's founding violence, and how its shadow persists.

title: The Sellout
author: Paul Beatty
desc: A Black man in a fictional LA ghetto reinstates slavery and segregation in his own town. The most savage and funniest satire on race in America ever written. Booker Prize winner — the first American ever to win it.

title: The Shadow of the Sun
author: Ryszard Kapuściński
desc: The great Polish foreign correspondent spent 40 years in Africa. This is his masterwork — a series of vivid dispatches from Ghana, Nigeria, Ethiopia, Rwanda, Angola and more. One of the best books ever written about the continent by an outsider who truly listened.

title: The Shadow of the Wind
author: Carlos Ruiz Zafón
desc: A boy finds a mysterious novel in a secret library and sets out to find its reclusive author — entering a labyrinth of mystery, love and tragedy in Franco's Barcelona. A genuine page-turner.

title: The Silk Roads
author: Peter Frankopan
desc: A new history of the world told through the trade routes connecting China to Rome to Africa. Frankopan recentres world history away from Europe — eye-opening and endlessly fascinating. Dense but very readable.

title: The Sixth Extinction
author: Elizabeth Kolbert
desc: Pulitzer Prize winner. Kolbert travels to the Great Barrier Reef, the Amazon, the Andes, fossil beds in Nevada and a frozen seed bank in Iceland to report on the sixth mass extinction — which is happening now, driven by us. Reads like travel writing, not a lecture. Each chapter focuses on a different species and a different corner of the world. Urgent, beautifully written and genuinely eye-opening about the scale of what is being lost.

title: The Stranger
author: Albert Camus
desc: Meursault, a detached French Algerian, shoots an Arab on a beach and faces trial — but really faces judgment for his refusal to feel. Camus's defining existentialist novella. Short, strange and enormously influential. Reads in a sitting.

title: The Swallows of Kabul
author: Yasmina Khadra
desc: Four lives in Taliban-controlled Kabul — two couples whose fates intertwine through a public execution. Short and devastating. One of the most powerful novels about life under the Taliban.

title: The Sympathizer
author: Viet Thanh Nguyen
desc: A communist spy embedded with South Vietnamese refugees confesses his story to a camp commandant. Pulitzer Prize winner. A brilliant, dark, funny novel about the Vietnam War told from the Vietnamese side — essential counter-history.

title: The Unbearable Lightness of Being
author: Milan Kundera
desc: Two couples navigate love, politics and the 1968 Prague Spring in Communist Czechoslovakia. Deeply romantic and deeply philosophical — but always gripping. One of the great European novels.

title: The Vegetarian
author: Han Kang
desc: A South Korean woman decides to stop eating meat, setting off a violent reaction from her family. Short, disturbing and beautifully strange. Han Kang's 2024 Nobel Prize made her essential reading. Start here.

title: The Warmth of Other Suns
author: Isabel Wilkerson
desc: The great migration of six million Black Americans from the South told through three extraordinary individual lives. Reads like a novel. One of the defining works of American narrative non-fiction — about race, hope and the search for a better life.

title: The White Tiger
author: Aravind Adiga
desc: Balram Halwai narrates his rise from village poverty to entrepreneurship in Delhi, by any means necessary. Dark, funny and ferociously readable. Booker Prize winner.

title: The Wind-Up Bird Chronicle
author: Haruki Murakami
desc: Toru Okada searches for his missing cat and wife, falling into a dreamlike world of dark secrets and alternative histories. Murakami's masterpiece — stranger, bigger and more ambitious than Norwegian Wood. For when you want to go deep.

title: The Yacoubian Building
author: Alaa Al Aswany
desc: A Cairo apartment block becomes a portrait of modern Egypt — its corruption, religious extremism, class tensions and sexual hypocrisy. The best-selling Arabic novel of its era. Readable, scandalous and illuminating.

title: The Year of Magical Thinking
author: Joan Didion
desc: Joan Didion's account of the year after her husband died suddenly at the dinner table. The clearest-eyed, most honest account of grief ever written. National Book Award winner. Short, precise and utterly unforgettable.

title: Things Fall Apart
author: Chinua Achebe
desc: The great African novel. Colonisation arrives in an Igbo village told from the inside — through the proud, flawed warrior Okonkwo. Short, powerful, and will completely shift how you think about empire and civilisation. The most widely read African book in the world.

title: Things We Lost in the Fire
author: Mariana Enriquez
desc: Twelve horror stories set in contemporary Argentina — but the real monsters are poverty, machismo, and the country's brutal history of disappeared people. Dark, original and genuinely frightening.

title: This Earth of Mankind
author: Pramoedya Ananta Toer
desc: Set in colonial Java at the turn of the 20th century — a young Javanese man studying under the Dutch falls in love with Nyai Ontosoroh, a woman of extraordinary intelligence and dignity living outside the law. Pramoedya Ananta Toer was Indonesia's greatest writer; he composed this novel aloud to fellow prisoners on Buru Island, where he was held without trial for over a decade. Indonesia is the world's fourth most populous country and one of the most overlooked in world literature.

title: To Kill a Mockingbird
author: Harper Lee
desc: The defining American novel about racial injustice and moral courage — told through the eyes of young Scout Finch as her father Atticus defends a Black man in 1930s Alabama. Warm, funny and devastating. One of the most readable classics ever written.

title: Tracks
author: Robyn Davidson
desc: Robyn Davidson set off alone across 2,700 kilometres of Australian desert with four camels and her dog. This memoir of that journey is about solitude, landscape, Aboriginal culture and what it means to be truly free.

title: True History of the Kelly Gang
author: Peter Carey
desc: Ned Kelly tells his own story — in barely punctuated, grammatically wild prose that perfectly captures the voice of a man who never had proper schooling. Booker Prize winner. A masterpiece of Australian voice — brutal, funny and heartbreaking.

title: Watership Down
author: Richard Adams
desc: A group of rabbits flee their warren's destruction and set out to found a new home. Sounds gentle — is actually a gripping, mythic adventure with real tension and danger. One of the great English novels, loved by children and adults alike. Warm, funny and impossible to put down.

title: We Need to Talk About Kevin
author: Lionel Shriver
desc: A mother writes letters to her estranged husband trying to understand how her son became a school shooter. Orange Prize winner. Deeply uncomfortable and utterly compelling — one of the most psychologically intense novels written about American violence and parenthood.

title: We Should All Be Feminists
author: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie
desc: Adichie's essential short essay on feminism — personal, funny and clear. Only 64 pages, reads in an hour. Given to every 16-year-old in Sweden. One of the most widely read essays of the 21st century.

title: Weep Not, Child
author: Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o
desc: The first English-language novel published by an East African writer. Njoroge clings to the hope of education as the Mau Mau rebellion tears Kenya apart around him. Short, powerful and quietly heartbreaking — an essential East African voice.

title: When Breath Becomes Air
author: Paul Kalanithi
desc: A neurosurgeon diagnosed with terminal lung cancer at 36 writes about mortality, medicine and meaning. One of the most beautiful books about death ever written — which is somehow also a book about living fully. Reads in one sitting and will change how you think about time.

title: Wide Sargasso Sea
author: Jean Rhys
desc: The prequel to Jane Eyre told from the perspective of Bertha Mason — the 'madwoman in the attic' — reimagined as Antoinette Cosway, a Creole woman in colonial Jamaica. Rhys tears apart the original novel and reassembles it from the colonised, female point of view. Only 156 pages, but one of the most powerful postcolonial novels ever written. Essential.

title: Zorba the Greek
author: Nikos Kazantzakis
desc: A bookish narrator arrives in Crete to work a mine and falls under the spell of the exuberant, life-devouring Zorba. One of the great novels about the joy of living — funny, philosophical and set in a Greece most tourists never see. Irresistibly life-affirming.
